caption:
Subscribe! Help us to reach 5000 subscribers!

#wtc #911 #truth #neverforget #usa
audio_track_actual: Single narrator over footage of WTC 7's collapse intercut with comparison footage of the 2017 Plasco Building collapse in Tehran.
video_overlay_text_observed: Side-by-side video comparison between WTC 7 and the Plasco Building collapse; on-screen text noting that WTC 7 fell 'at free fall speed perfectly into its footprint' and labeling WTC 7 the third steel building 'to ever collapse from fire.'
audio_content_summary: The narrator advances four claims about World Trade Center 7. First, that the building collapsed at free-fall speed into its own footprint (NIST has acknowledged a ~2.25-second pure-free-fall period in NCSTAR 1A; see 2001-wtc-collapse-physics-001). Second, that WTC 7 is one of only three steel-framed high-rises in history to have collapsed from fire — the other two being WTC 1 and 2 on the same day. The Plasco Building collapse in Tehran on January 19, 2017 is a documented fourth case; the narrator notes Plasco fell asymmetrically and uses it as a contrasting reference for what an unrigged fire-induced collapse looks like. Third, that Larry Silverstein's September 2002 PBS America Rebuilds statement — 'maybe the smartest thing to do is pull it' — was an instruction to demolish the building, an interpretation Silverstein's spokesperson disputed and that NIST's final report did not adopt (see 2002-silverstein-pull-it-001). Fourth, that the rapidity with which the attacks were officially attributed indicates pre-attack preparation rather than post-attack investigation.
implied_frame: The reel pairs the physics-of-collapse claim with the 'pull it' semantic argument to advance the controlled-demolition reading of WTC 7. Each underlying fact (the collapse time, the Silverstein statement, the Plasco comparison) is documented; the synthesized inference — that WTC 7 was pre-rigged for demolition — is the contested editorial proposition NIST's WTC 7 final report directly rejects.
capture_method: Headless browser recording of the in-page <video> element via MediaRecorder over captureStream(), viewport 1280x800.
